skill.name: test-driven-development
skill.description: Guides agents to use test-driven development for features, bug fixes, refactoring, and behavior changes.
skill.summary: Write a failing test first, implement the smallest passing change, then refactor while tests stay green.
skill.author: DYAI2025

security_audit.summary: AI review found no evidence of malware, data exfiltration, prompt injection, weak cryptography, or network reconnaissance in SKILL.md. Most static findings are false positives from Markdown code fences and example text. The only real risk factor is guidance to run local npm test commands, which is expected for a TDD skill but should remain user-controlled.

low_findings.title: Local Test Command Guidance
low_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to run local npm test commands to verify red and green TDD states. This is a legitimate development workflow, but it is still external command execution and should use the project test runner only.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain explicit local npm test commands and expected output. They do not include shell interpolation, downloads, credential access, or arbitrary command construction.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives in Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography and network reconnaissance hits do not correspond to actionable code in SKILL.md. They appear in plain documentation, Markdown fences, tables, or test examples with no executable malicious behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of these lines found instructional prose and test-quality examples, not cryptographic code or network scanning. No executable network or crypto implementation is present in the skill file.

content.value_statement: Untested changes can pass review while hiding broken behavior. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through red-green-refactor steps with proof that each test fails first.
actual_capabilities: Enforces writing a focused failing test before implementation code.
Guides verification that the test fails for the expected reason.
Promotes minimal implementation work until the target test passes.
Provides examples of strong and weak test design patterns.
Uses a checklist to confirm tests, edge cases, and clean output before completion.
limitations: It is a process guide, not a test framework or code generator.
It assumes the project already has a runnable test setup.
It may slow exploratory work when requirements are still unclear.
It does not define language-specific testing libraries beyond examples.

use_cases.title: Add a New Feature Safely
use_cases.description: Write the desired behavior as a failing test, implement only what passes it, then refactor with confidence.

use_cases.title: Reproduce and Fix Bugs
use_cases.description: Capture the bug with a failing regression test before changing the code that caused the defect.

use_cases.title: Refactor Existing Code
use_cases.description: Add behavior tests around risky code so cleanup work preserves the current contract.

prompt_templates.title: Start a TDD Change
prompt_templates.prompt: Use test-driven development for this change. First identify one behavior, write the smallest failing test, and stop before implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Verify the Red Step
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this failing test result. Confirm whether it fails for the expected reason before any production code is written.

prompt_templates.title: Implement the Green Step
prompt_templates.prompt: Now write only the smallest production change needed to pass the failing test. Do not add unrelated behavior or refactors.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Regression Suite
prompt_templates.prompt: Use TDD to build a focused regression suite for this bug or refactor. Sequence the tests from simplest behavior to edge cases.

output_examples.input: Request to fix a validation bug where empty email values are accepted.
output_examples.output: A failing regression test is written first, the failure reason is verified, then a minimal validation change is made.

output_examples.input: Request to add retry behavior to an operation helper.
output_examples.output: A single behavior test defines the retry count and success case before the helper implementation is added.

output_examples.input: Request to refactor a complex module with weak coverage.
output_examples.output: The current behavior is protected with tests before internal cleanup begins, and tests stay green after each change.

best_practices: Run the new test before implementation and confirm it fails for the intended behavior.
Keep each test focused on one observable behavior with a clear name.
Refactor only after the target test and related tests are passing cleanly.
anti_patterns: Writing production code first and adding tests only after it appears to work.
Keeping exploratory code as reference while pretending the next test is first.
Testing mocks or implementation details instead of externally visible behavior.

faq.question: Does this skill write tests for me?
faq.answer: It guides the agent to write tests first, verify failure, implement minimal code, and refactor safely.

faq.question: Can I use it for bug fixes?
faq.answer: Yes. The recommended flow is to reproduce the bug with a failing test before changing production code.

faq.question: Does it support non-JavaScript projects?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow is language independent, although the examples use TypeScript and npm test commands.

faq.question: What if the test passes immediately?
faq.answer: The skill treats that as a signal that the test does not prove new behavior and should be revised.

faq.question: Is this useful for refactoring?
faq.answer: Yes. It encourages adding behavior tests before refactoring so internal changes keep the same contract.

faq.question: Does it run external commands?
faq.answer: It recommends running local project test commands. Users should review commands before execution.
